analogy: Think of "Rebels and the Raj 1857" through the chapter's own opening images: THEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 258 Late in the afternoon of 10 May 1857, the sepoys in the cantonment of Meerut broke out in mutiny. It began in the lines of the native infantry, spread very swiftly to the cavalry and then to... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Rebels and the Raj 1857 is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that tHEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 258 Late in the afternoon of 10 May 1857, the sepoys in the cantonment of Meerut broke out in mutiny.

The text explains that it began in the lines of the native infantry, spread very swiftly to the cavalry and then to the city.

The text explains that the ordinary people of the town and surrounding villages joined the sepoys.

The text explains that the sepoys captured the bell of arms where the arms and ammunition were kept and proceeded to attack white people, and to ransack and burn their bungalows and property.

The text explains that government buildings – the record office, jail, court, post office, treasury, etc. – were destroyed and plundered.

The text explains that as darkness descended, a group of sepoys rode off towards Delhi.

The text explains that the sepoys arrived at the gates of the Red Fort early in the morning on 11 May.

The text explains that it was the month of Ramzan, the Muslim holy month of prayer and fasting.

The text explains that the old Mughal emperor, Bahadur Shah, had just finished his prayers and meal before the sun rose and the fast began.

The text explains that the sepoys who had gathered under his window told him: “We have come from Meerut after killing all the Englishmen there, because they asked us to bite bullets that were coated with the fat of cows and pigs with our teeth.

The text explains that this has corrupted the faith of Hindus and Muslims alike.’’ Another group of sepoys also entered Delhi, and the ordinary people of the city joined them.

The text explains that europeans were killed in large numbers; the rich of Delhi were attacked and looted.

The text explains that it was clear that Delhi had gone out of British control.

The text explains that some sepoys rode into the Red Fort, without observing the elaborate court etiquette expected of them.

The text explains that they demanded that the emperor give them his blessings.

The text explains that surrounded by the sepoys, Bahadur Shah had no other option but to comply.

The text explains that source 1 Ordinary life in extraordinary times What happened in the cities during the months of the revolt?

The text explains that the direction of the business is in able hands.” The tahsildar was later identified as the principal rebel leader of Bijnor.

The text explains that it is difficult on the basis of the available documents to provide direct answers to such questions.

The text explains that source 2 REBELS AND THE RAJ Mutiny – a collective disobedience of rules and regulations within the armed forces Revolt – a rebellion of people against established authority and power.

The text explains that in Jhansi, the rani was forced by the popular pressure around her to assume the leadership of the u p r i s i n g .

The text explains that (c) The ally would have to provide the resources for maintaining this contingent.

The text explains that resident was the designation of a representative of the Governor General who lived in a state which was not under direct British rule.

The text explains that source 3 Ü Read the entire section and discuss why people mourned the departure of Wajid Ali Shah.

The text explains that now, under the British, the peasant was directly exposed to overassessment of revenue and inflexible methods of collection.

The text explains that rEBELS AND THE RAJ Source 4 What taluqdars thought The attitude of the taluqdars was best expressed by Hanwant Singh, the Raja of Kalakankar, near Rae Bareli.

The text explains that while these sources reveal the minds of officials, they tell us very little about what the rebels wanted. Ü Discuss...

The text explains that rEBELS AND THE RAJ The Azamgarh Proclamation, 25 August 1857 This is one of the main sources of our knowledge about what the rebels wanted: It is well known to all, that in this age the people of Hindostan, both Hindoos and Mohammedans, are being ruined under the tyranny and the oppression of the infidel and treacherous English.

The text explains that therefore it is incumbent on all to give up the hope of the continuation of the British sway, side with me, and deserve the consideration of the Badshahi, or imperial government, by their individual exertion in Source 5 contd

The text explains that if the religion of a Hindoo or Mussalman is lost, what remains in the world? Ü Compare the reasons for the mutiny as stated in the arzi with those mentioned by the taluqdar (Source 3).

The text explains that source 6 3.2 Against the symbols of oppression The proclamations completely rejected everything associated with British rule or firangi raj as they called it.

The text explains that source 7 Ü What, according to this account, were the problems faced by the British in dealing with these villagers?

The text explains that 5.3 Vengeance and retribution As waves of anger and shock spread in Britain, demands for retribution grew louder.

The text explains that examine any two sources presented in the chapter, choosing one visual and one text, and discuss how these represent the point of view of the victor and the vanquished.

The text explains that do all the sources say the same thing, or are there differences?

The text explains that refer to Maps 1 and 2 and plot the areas where the revolt was most widespread.

The text explains that how close or far were these areas from the colonial cities?

The text explains that read a biography of any one of the leaders of the revolt of 1857.

The text explains that do these include government reports, newspaper accounts, stories in regional languages, visual material, anything else?

The text explains that see a film made on the revolt of 1857 and write about the way it represents the revolt.

The text explains that how does it depict the British, the rebels, and those who remained loyal to the British?

The text explains that what does it say about peasants, city dwellers, tribals, zamindars and taluqdars?

The text explains that what kind of a response does the film seek to evoke?

The text explains that you could visit: http://books.google.com (for accounts of 1857 by British officials) www.copsey-family.org/allenc/ lakshmibai/links.html (for letters of Rani Lakshmibai)

Source: NCERT Class 12 Themes in Indian History III, Chapter 2: Rebels and the Raj 1857

prelims_traps: Do not treat THEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 258 and THEME as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Rebels and the Raj 1857; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Rebels and the Raj 1857 through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use THEMES IN INDIAN HISTORY – PART III 258 and THEME to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
